sets.passage: One evening, on my way home, I noticed an elderly man standing at a busy crossroads, looking utterly (1)___. While we waited, I learned that his name was Mr. Reed and that he had once been a (11)___. Eventually, a worried young woman — his granddaughter — arrived, having searched for him for (12)___. The relief and gratitude on both their faces was something I will never (13)___. It had cost me only an hour of my evening, yet to that family it had meant (14)___. sets.passage: My younger brother, Leo, has always been a (1)___. He takes apart old radios, clocks and toys just to see how they (2)___. sets.passage: When I was younger, I could not bear (1)___. The slightest criticism of my work felt like a personal (2)___, and I would react with anger or hurt, defending myself instead of (3)___. This habit, I now see, held me back for (4)___. Convinced I was always right, I missed countless chances to (5)___. The change began with a wise art teacher. After I had angrily dismissed her suggestion about a painting, she said something I have never (6)___: 'A person who cannot hear criticism cannot (7)___.' Her calm words struck me deeply. I began, slowly and painfully, to practise listening to feedback without immediately (8)___ myself. It was incredibly hard; my first instinct was always to argue, but I forced myself instead to ask, 'Is there any (9)___ in what they say?' To my surprise, there usually (10)___. Most criticism, I discovered, was not an attack but a (11)___ — a chance to see a weakness I was blind to. The people who pointed out my mistakes were often doing me a (12)___, even when it stung. As I learned to welcome honest feedback rather than fear it, I improved far (13)___ than ever before. Today, I actively (14)___ criticism, knowing it is one of the fastest ways to grow. sets.passage: When my school paired me with a (1)___ in a distant country, I expected little more than a boring assignment. We were supposed to exchange letters in English to practise the (2)___. Through them, I came to know a daily life utterly (5)___ from my own: different food, different weather, different (6)___. At first, the differences were what struck me most. But the longer we wrote, the more I noticed how (7)___ we truly were. We laughed at the same silly things, worried about the same exams, and shared the same hopes for the (8)___. Aisha gently corrected my clumsy assumptions about her country, and I did the (9)___ for her about mine. Slowly, a real friendship grew across the thousands of miles between (10)___. The news, which had once shown me only frightening images of faraway places, now felt (11)___, because in one of those places lived my friend. When we finally met in person years later, it felt as though we had known each other all our (12)___. That simple school assignment taught me something no textbook could. sets.passage: For my grandmother's eightieth birthday, I wanted to give her something truly (1)___, not just bought from a shop. I had imagined it would be (4)___. It was not. My first lump of clay collapsed the moment the wheel began to (5)___. My second flew off completely. Slowly, over many weeks, my hands began to (9)___ the clay instead of fighting it. The bowl I finally made was far from (10)___ — its sides were uneven and its glaze a little patchy. By a shop's standards, it was clumsy. But every small (11)___ in it held a memory of the hours I had spent (12)___ it. When I gave it to my grandmother, she held it as if it were made of (13)___.
